rows.title: Egypt National AI Strategy: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology Sets Framework for Artificial Intelligence Development
rows.summary:
Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (MCIT) has published the Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, a non-binding policy framework establishing priorities and objectives for artificial intelligence development across the Egyptian economy and public sector.

On 2021, the MCIT released the Egypt National AI Strategy, setting out the government's approach to positioning Egypt as a regional hub for artificial intelligence research, adoption and governance. The strategy was developed under the broader mandate of the Digital Egypt initiative and aligns with the country's Vision 2030 development agenda.

The strategy identifies four principal pillars: building AI readiness through infrastructure investment and data availability; developing human capital via education, training and talent pipelines; fostering an enabling environment through regulatory frameworks and public-private partnerships; and promoting AI adoption across priority sectors including healthcare, agriculture, education, transport and financial services. The MCIT stated that the strategy aims to leverage Egypt's demographic profile, including a large youth population, as a driver of AI-sector growth.

The strategy designates the MCIT as the lead coordinating authority for implementation, with sector-specific ministries responsible for adoption within their respective domains. The document also references Egypt's engagement with international bodies, including the African Union's continental AI frameworks and cooperation with the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) on AI ethics principles. No binding legal obligations are imposed on private sector entities under the strategy document itself; it functions as a policy roadmap rather than primary legislation or a binding regulatory instrument. Specific regulatory measures flowing from the strategy, including any data governance or sector-specific AI rules, are expected to be addressed through subsequent legislative and regulatory instruments under Egypt's existing legal framework.

rows.title: Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology Publishes National AI Governance Framework Guide
rows.summary:
Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, acting through the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing, and Emerging Technologies, has published the Guide to Egypt's National AI Governance Framework (Version 2.0), authorised on 15 March 2026 by Minister Raafat Hindy, establishing a formal governance architecture for artificial intelligence across Egypt's public and private sectors.

On 15 March 2026, the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing, and Emerging Technologies released the Guide, prepared by the Egyptian centre for Responsible AI (ECRAI). The document underwent a structured drafting process beginning in August 2025, incorporating a public consultation phase in January 2026 and feedback integration from government bodies, private sector participants and international organisations before final release. The Guide states that no financial penalties are associated with this publication.

The Framework sets out a governance philosophy the Guide terms the "State as Orchestrator," under which the state is described as simultaneously enabling AI adoption through infrastructure, data provision and capacity building, while establishing regulatory protections for high-risk systems. The Guide defines a hierarchy of instruments: the overarching Framework suite, which encompasses the National Strategy, the Guide itself, National Guidelines for Trustworthy and Responsible AI, and future sectoral regulations. The Guide characterises itself as the strategic document responsible for defining what is governed, including risk tier classifications, a "Dual-Check" compliance logic, and an institutional architecture for enforcement and oversight. The Framework states it applies to all entities operating AI systems within Egypt's public and private sectors.

The Guide further describes Egypt's approach as a "Strategic Hybrid" model, combining elements of enabling innovation with targeted safeguards for applications assessed as high-risk. According to ECRAI, compliance pathways include ethical impact assessments and technical audits. The document positions Egypt as a conduit between the Arab region, Africa and the global digital economy, and the Guide states that AI is recognised as a strategic national priority within Egypt's constitutional framework. The Guide is classified as a public document with applicability to all sectors.
rows.body_name: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology

rows.title: NTRA and EG-CERT Launch Internet Safety Awareness Campaign in Egypt
rows.summary:
Egypt's National Telecom Regulatory Authority, acting through its national cybersecurity centre EG-CERT, launched a public awareness campaign on safe internet use, targeting multiple segments of Egyptian society and coinciding with Safer Internet Day in February 2026.

On 25 February 2026, the National Telecom Regulatory Authority (NTRA) of Egypt, operating under the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, launched the campaign alongside the debut of the digital citizenship and online protection platform www.wa3i.net. The NTRA reports that the launch was attended by the Minister of Communications and Information Technology, Eng. Raouf Handey, as well as the Chairperson of the National Council for Childhood and Motherhood, representatives from UNICEF Egypt, and the resident representative of the United Nations Development Programme, together with leadership from the Ministry and the National Council for Childhood and Motherhood.

The NTRA states that the campaign is designed to promote a culture of safe and responsible technology use across social media platforms, raise awareness of the importance of protecting personal data, and reduce risks associated with the digital environment. The authority notes that campaign materials focus on reinforcing values of respect and positive engagement on digital platforms, emphasise the active role of parents in monitoring their children's internet use, and highlight the importance of responsible use of social media. The NTRA further states that the initiative forms part of its broader efforts to address growing challenges in the digital world, equipping users with practical guidance on personal data protection, countering electronic fraud, mitigating cybersecurity risks, and building a society capable of engaging more consciously with technology.

The NTRA describes the wa3i.net platform as among the first integrated Arabic-language platforms specialising in digital citizenship and online protection, providing an interactive and educational space with Arabic content dedicated to targeted demographic groups, including children, adolescents, youth, parents, teachers, and older persons, categorised by age group.
rows.body_name: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology
